title: Notes on Geometric Perception for Large-Scale Robotic Systems
summary: A short reflection on why geometric consistency, calibration, and uncertainty matter when perception systems leave controlled lab environments.

sections.heading: Why geometry still matters
paragraphs: Large-scale robotic perception is often described through sensors and algorithms, but the practical bottleneck is usually geometric consistency. A system may collect dense point clouds, accurate poses, or high-resolution images, yet the final reconstruction depends on whether all observations share a reliable spatial reference.
For example, a rigid transformation can be written inline as \( T \in SE(3) \), which is compact but still makes the assumed motion model explicit.

This is especially visible in aircraft surface reconstruction, mobile scanning, and structured field environments. Small calibration errors can become visible as global deformation, misalignment, or unstable downstream estimation.
A simple registration objective can be expressed as \[ \min_{R,t} \sum_i \left\| \mathbf{p}_i - (R\mathbf{q}_i + t) \right\|^2 \], where the residuals summarize point-wise geometric consistency.

sections.heading: Useful habits
points: Treat calibration as part of the perception pipeline, not as a one-time preprocessing step.
Record coordinate frames, transformation directions, and units explicitly in code and experiment logs.
Evaluate global consistency with independent checks instead of relying only on local residuals.

sections.heading: Open direction
paragraphs: A useful research direction is to connect geometric optimization with uncertainty-aware decision making. Instead of producing a single fused result, the system can expose where the reconstruction is reliable and where additional observations are needed.
